item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Just giving credit were credit is due should be enough

Unfortunately, that's not feasible presently. We remove things that pose a legal threat to the continued existence of the wiki, and non CC compliant images do fall under that category, I'm afraid. Adding in links to the original would just be breaking CC compliance with extra steps.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
First link not woring

The others don't contain the images or the images are hidden somewere within the page

I undertand taing out the pictures but could we please get easy access to them? So at least we get to imagine how the article originaly looked like. I actualy find it a bit sad that we have to butcher articles like this in order to avoid persecution. Just giving credit were credit is due should be enouth

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fence is suposed to keep people from finding out the SCP

They found no survivors but found several cadavers. The few surivors weren't in the town proper

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I miss seeing SCP like this, no distracting pictures or "fancy" formatting just words on a page dripping with terror and intrigue, wonderfully built world here.

item.title: Re: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: No worries, this was posted before image copyright was a big deal for the wiki, and if images have to be removed, so be it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: owl city is fun music

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: all the years this has been up and there's been no large list of references made in the comments. i feel i should check that SCP declassified sub

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused and terrified.
I think this is the first time I've come across something like a concept-salad, a distant cousin of the word-salad. There's enough unity of theme that I sort of understand the framework of what's happening, but enough randomness and insanity that it's still terrifying and unknowable.
I do agree with the comment above me on the Sheogorath part, though. Seeing that made me horribly confused and wonder if the other named creatures were pop culture references. I even Googled "the dark deer dm'axx" to no avail. Took me out of the immersion a little, but frankly not for long. Congratulations, here's a +1 and a new record for my personal most-WTF-face-inducing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I used to really enjoy this SCP, and the images really made it better and gave it more effect before they were removed, however looking back at it there are some issues.

There are too many pop culture references, and it seems very silly at some points. The Sheogorath reference is lazy and uninspired to be quite frank, and the owl city part doesn't make much sense either.

Look, I know it's supposed to be eldritch fuckery and all, but you can't just include random, inane bullshit like that for the hell of it. Lovecraftian eldritch horrors aren't going to somehow make it so that music from the future plays.

There's plenty that can be done to fix this up, make it more horrifying, but I doubt that that's going to be done with the way the site is now, and I doubt that there's going to be any replacement images.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i upvoted for the sheer level of detail in the worldbuilding of this article, references aside. gonna need new images to really get its punch back, though, since they've all been removed.

also i definitely need the context for that owl city reference explained.

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image belongs to Cor Jaring. It is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 due to a lack of a Creative Commons license.

The second image belongs to Benedetta Bonichi. It is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 due to a lack of a Creative Commons license.

The third image was taken by the Soviet Union's Venera spacecraft. Licensing Staff will follow a precedent of not using this image due to images related to the Venera projects being under an ambiguous status.

The fourth image belongs to Winsor McCay. After discussing with other Licensing Staff member, we have decided to remove the image due to its ambiguous public domain status due to being a political cartoon.

The last image could not be source reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, everyone likes Lovecraft style guerrilla but this article has many flaws. First of all, the tone is a bit off, especially in the description of logs. There are at least three spacial anomalies and only one is described.
A lot of things happening, almost no detail about most of them.
I think that the electrical fence enclosing 3.14km^2 is pointless…
In the description there is this sentence:

No survivors from the anomalous events which affected Daleport have been found within the town proper, but the remains of a large number of Daleport citizens have been recovered.

And the 90+ survivors are not mentioned until later in a collapsible.

Dubstep (and its non-contextualized time anomaly).

For these reasons, -1

PS: also, I don't know if I liked the cabbage deity or the goat-ball

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As noted before, time doesn't seem to work all too properly in Daleport. Especially when you consider the bit about a dubstep song being heard in the background 15 years before actually being written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's cause I love lovecraft and didn't catch a lot of the pop culture references others seem to be complaining about, but I absolutely adore this article. Creepy, bizarre things happening in a place is always a fun time for me, and I really like what you do with Pangloss. I like that what you initially think is the source of the problem, all the mist, is in fact trying to save everyone. The speech in the end, where the Reverend explains that he called down all the gods so that there would only be a single victor who would establish a consistent reality, is a fascinating theme to center the article on. Wish I could learn about those robots to be honest. Is that the Church of the Broken God working with the Foundation, or am I totally off?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Sheogorath remains, ruling his kingdom of two faces before he himself brings its downfall, only to be stopped by a great prisoner of the dragon king.

Wait, what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too busy, and with silly pop culture references. Time displaced musical score's are even a little cringe worthy. Creepy dubstep and Sheogorath? Just no.

Still a fun read, but feels more like Final Fantasy than it does Lovecraft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the exact same way,i wanted this to expand to make more scp's but idk what their gonna do,i really wanted a scp of the god with the bowler hat cause he was really interesting in my opinion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idk why but i like this scp way more than any other because of the amount of detail it has. And the "gods" themselves that were summoned were really cool in my opinion like the one in the bowler hat really has me interested and i wish it had its own file

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i dont know what i am my throat goes somewhere else. my throat goes somewhere else and i do not like where it goes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: According to the wiki, Sheogorath was first mentioned/identified by name in ESII, which was release October of 1996. So I guess it's possible. But it seems he had the biggest presence in IV, which was released in the March of 2006. Though, even with that, I would ask: would a priest from what appears to be small town in the US be familiar with a fantasy video game like Elder Scrolls? Familiar enough to include it in his world-saving plan? I wouldn't have thought so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was being put off by the references myself but then a thought occurred to me. The whole thing was an attempt to get various horrendous entities to wipe each other out while keeping them contained to Daleport. Could the Reverend have been trying to tie them down to names and roles to help do so? And, doing so, could he not have resorted to using Lovecraft and Elder Scrolls etc references as a shortcut?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know you and yet I love you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, so I know this is literally years after the fact, but I am still giggling over the multiple comments about a Oblivion reference, but none at all about direct quotes from Pokemon 2000: The Movie?

The Power of One begins with believing! (said Reverend Michael Hawshore.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this SCP and consider it one of my favourites, but there's one sentence that's always nagged me. It's this:

Sheogorath remains, ruling his kingdom of two faces before he himself brings its downfall, only to be stopped by a great prisoner of the dragon king.

Why is there an Elder Scrolls reference here? Did James Curtis play Daggerfall and like the Daedric Prince of Madness so much that he decided to include him in a book? Did he foresee the plot of the Shivering Isles and include it for a laugh?

If not, does this imply that the events of the Elder Scrolls games, or at least those of Oblivion's Shivering Isles DLC, actually happened? Are Bethesda actually a group of thaumaturgists peering into other realities and using their historical events as video game plots? Does this mean that the Fallout universe is real too?

item.title: Great potential completely killed by mood-breaking references
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love the core of this skip. I'm totally into the whole Lovecraftian cult, gods battling gods, unfathomable otherworldly conflict thing it's got going. But it seemed like every other line was a painfully obvious reference to something completely unrelated. It's like I was watching a great horror movie where a random cameo would jump on screen every few scenes and wiggle their eyebrows at the camera before immediately dying. It interrupts everything and kills any mood that would have been set otherwise.

If a reference adds something to the story, awesome. Do it. If a reference is actually relevant and expands on the reader's understanding of the setting, amazing. Do it. But if your reference is nothing but a non-sequitur — please, leave it out. It's painful to read.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only complain is that it MUST be expanded. Daleport could be the source for so many other SCPs, tales, and a slowly progressing explanation of some of what happened there.

By the way, it could be interesting to hint about other survivors leaving the city, bringing their anomaly with them, and to develop on the refugees rescued by Pangloss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The non-committal statements are warranted here. The Foundation has almost zero direct information on the actual course of events- all they can say for certain is that something really screwed up happened to reality in this town. Anything else is various degrees of theory and speculation based on forensic analysis of fragmentary evidence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm okay with the references and Lovecraft stuff, but I can't abide the gross excess of non-committal description. "Appears", "seems", "is believed"… Stop being wishy-washy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And now the weather :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Twenty goats stuck together making a goat ball, spin round and round all the way to goat hell.

Sounds like someone played Goat Simulator. Queen + Demon = getting ragdolled out of reality.

Anyway, the references were too heavy-handed to me. Sorry. Remove the skyrim reference at least, ideally do something else other than the lovecraftian language too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article is just too busy for me. Too much stuff going on that doesn't feel connected to each other, is tonally inconsistent in places, and drags on too long, making it feel like a chore to finish by the Reverend's journal entries. The R'lyeh speak near the beginning really sucked me out of it and then I never really came back from that, in part because nothing else after that is very enticing.

Sorry, but -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely loved this! I got the implication that this is implying a kind of cosmic war going on, with Earth being ground zero. Being a diehard writer of weird fiction, the Lovecraftian elements themselves were enough to win me over, even if it wasn't an already intriguing idea that was executed wonderfully. I also appreciate that you included just enough ridiculousness (Owl City comes to mind) in here to make it just that much more fun to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Djrr, the Dark God of Lamp-Posts, The Blind One illuminating the way for his followers,

Yni-Yni, the Trepanner, frees his followers from the skulls of their oppressors.

The Shredded Cabbage of Misfortune will destroy those who oppose the vegetable monarchy.

Zinn, Herald of Maarp commands the army of Lepidoptera to carry those away who sleep with the flowers beneath the Silver Ash in the Dream Attics of Inanimatum.

These need to appear in another tale somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No-vote.

I really wasn't feeling this skip, but then the explanation of what was going on at the end saved this from a downvote. Seriously, that's a really neat idea, I just wasn't a fan of the execution - felt too much like Lovecraft, or Stephen King's The Mist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I've always thought Lovecraft had contact with some anomalous being in the Foundationverse. That'd explain it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone needs to make a tale of this. Please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for the idea of having to periodically cull the number of gods, and for a literal god of containment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noticed Maarp, but for some reason hadn't noticed those two others despite being right next to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Twenty goats stuck together making a goat ball, spin round and round all the way to goat hell.

+1

I probably didn't get most of the references but this skip is beautiful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's some incongruity here that pulls me out of the article - dubstep, shredded cabbage, etcetera.

Can't upvote, so neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Zinn, Herald of Maarp commands the army of Lepidoptera

I see what you did there.

But this is really cool. The giant eldritch showdown of ultimate destiny is great. The anomalous beings are great. The creepy is great. Pangloss is great. great great great great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm seeing a lot of bashing on this article, but I have to say I extremely enjoyed it. While I get the general gist of the scip (what happens when a town summons gods to fight each other) its not clear what everything is doing. We know he who walks under dreams is unalligned but what's his story? What is he even doing there except fighting monstrosities? What are all the other small time players doing in this, where do they fit in? And who the heck is that giant arm creature?!

Admittedly, I did find it to be heavy on the lovecraftian influence. I do love how in depth and detailed this is though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know about this one. After reading the discussion, I'm starting to question my upvote. I don't think the SCP 'Verse and Lovecraft Mythos (I refuse to call it the Cthulhu Mythos for reasons I won't detail here) should interact outside of Project Crossover. Something can be Lovecraftian without being connected to Lovecraft (unless I don't know the meaning of "Lovecraftian"). This is plenty creepy, but I'm starting to like it less and less. I'll chalk it up to sleep deprivation for now, but I'll reach a verdict soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really dislike how reference heavy this got after the first draft. It was fine as a nice little modern-day Lovecraft Lite story; the references and in jokes to other parts of the wiki are really unnecessary and alienating.

Upvote cancelled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A nice subversion of the evil cultist stereotype, with an excellent exposition,also The Shredded Cabbage of Misfortune is a reference to Elrich?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great long article, very creepy pictures, references that I enjoyed. +1 great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Forgot to comment on how much I loved this. Long articles FTW. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The title of those gods reminded me of a Sequential Art Lovecraftian gods which had the titles of 'Defiler of Graves', 'That of Fetid Waters' and 'Whose Touch Turns All To Poo'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah screw it upvoted anyway. A cult that summons eldritch abominations to earth in order to trick them into fighting each other to the death is such an awesome subversion of the standard chaos god worshipping cult trope that I just can't dislike it. It's not too often you can reasonably argue that the lovecraftian cult are kind of the good guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to admit… this also stopped me from upvoting. I could take the Shredded Cabbage of Misfortune… I mean, whatever. But obvious video game references? I just personally couldn't take it. It broke my immersion into tiny little bits.

Granted, the whole thing is basically a Lovecraft reference, but there was a lot going for this. Incredibly creepy and evocative pictures, some decent to very good writing describing the horrors that befell the town, great pacing and build-up, and finally a twinge of genuine pathos for the extra-dimensional suffering of the cult leader. There's some junk cluttering up this SCP, but it's on the whole very good.

But, I'm sorry, the Oblivion allusions are the straw that broke this particular camel's back. Just couldn't take it. No upvote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sheogorath remains, ruling his kingdom of two faces before he himself brings its downfall, only to be stopped by a great prisoner of the dragon king.

I see what you did there.

One one hand I found it amusing and I'm happy on these rare occasions I manage to catch a reference…but I think it was maybe too silly and it broke my suspension of disbelief.

I've never read any Lovecraft so I guess maybe I didn't understand this SCP as well as I should. I found all the made-up words and deities to be too confusing for me to all keep track of. By the end, I didn't really understand it at all. I really don't get what happened to the town or why (but I guess maybe I'm not supposed to).

On the plus side, it was weird and creepy.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I will have to go over this later to catch all the references but this was an easy upvote for me. Lovecraft but not too Lovecraft.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A fucking lot of user references. Damn. And more! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I understand the temptation to make this an all out, unabashed meta brawl of the gods, but some of these new additions are just kinda making me groan. While this wasn't the most serious of SCPs in the first place, I'm switching my upvote to a no-vote. Shame, cause I was really digging this when I first read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why the first two expeditions are called "attempted", since they both came back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But that's the best part! :O

Seriously though, I'm just writing dumb stuff for fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this a lot but I'm not sure I can upvote with that blatant reference to the Elder Scrolls video game series. Seeing Sheogorath involved with an SCP is just too silly for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this. The only part I liked less was the Reverend's diary, which I find annoyingly cliche by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Enjoyed this a lot. +1
But where are the shoggoths?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For all the craziness and talk of magic things and anomalous happenings going on, there are very few articles on this site that do a good job of treating a human encounter with gods.

This is one of those. And I hope others will brave the length to enjoy all of it.

+1. Emphatically.
